name: Etteilla I
description:
# Etteilla I — Livre de Thot

> Part of the Etteilla lineage — see the hub at `10-etteilla.mdx`, then `10b` and `10c`.

## At a glance

The **original** and the genuine watershed: **Jean-Baptiste Alliette** ("Etteilla")
designs and publishes the **first tarot deck built expressly for divination** (1788–89).
Where every prior deck was a game pack later *read* as an oracle, this one was *born* an
oracle. This is the true birth-date of the divinatory tarot **object**.

## Origin & dating

Alliette (1738–1791) read ordinary cards professionally first, then — fired by de
Gébelin's 1781 Egyptian theory — built a tarot to match it. His method-books appear
**1783–85**; the deck, the **Grand Etteilla** / *Livre de Thot*, **1788–89**. He claimed
to "restore" the cards to their supposed Egyptian/hieroglyphic original.

## Provenance & evidence

The deck and books survive and are well reconstructed (Tarot Heritage; benebell wen). Its
departures from the Marseille are deliberate and documented:

- **Renumbered and reimagined trumps** — e.g. **Death is renumbered to 17**, breaking the
  standard order entirely.
- **Added cards** — querent/"etteilla" cards and the four **elements** as figures.
- **Upright/reversed keyword meanings** printed into the system — the ancestor of modern
  "card meanings."

## Game or divination?

**Divination — the first true case.** The grammar should mark **1788** as the birth of a
purpose-built divinatory deck (distinct from de Gébelin's 1781 birth of the *theory*).

## The fear question

**Conspicuously unafraid.** Etteilla ran a **"New School of Magic,"** advertised courses
in Paris papers, and signed his books. In late-18th-century Paris, cartomancy was
fashionable and lucrative. The literate male entrepreneur operated in broad daylight —
the sharpest contrast with the prosecuted, working-class fortune-tellers across the
Channel.

## Counter-voices

Etteilla is the original voice of the "recovered suppressed wisdom" story — he sincerely
(and profitably) presented his system as ancient Egyptian. Both things are true at once:
he **founded a real, lasting cartomantic practice** *and* propagated a **false Egyptian
pedigree**. Honour the first; flag the second.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- The renumbering and added cards have **no clean Marseille mapping** — preserve, do not
  normalise.
- Separate Alliette's own 1788–89 deck from the 1804 student reprint.

## Sources & further reading

- "Etteilla," Wikipedia; "Etteilla: The First Modern Card Reader," Tarot Heritage;
  benebell wen, Etteilla materials.

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13) — metadata correction.** The per-card `metadata.symbols` on the minor (pip) cards had been templated from **Rider-Waite-Smith (1909)** scene iconography (e.g. a 'sunflower'/'cat' on the Queen, 'bandage'/'infinity' on pips) — anachronistic for Etteilla's 1788 near-pip pack, whose minors are arranged suit-symbols with printed keyword titles, not Smith's narrative scenes. Those false symbol lists have been REMOVED. The cards' divinatory sections (Etteilla's own upright/reversed meanings) are unaffected. The `archetype: card:<rank>-of-<suit>` fields are kept only as structural cross-references (King of Bâtons ↔ King of Wands), not as a claim of shared imagery. See research/cards/<this deck>.md.

etymology.notes: Represents the male querent (significator). In Hermetic philosophy, this is the Ein Sof or Tao—divine consciousness before manifestation.
etymology.origin: From Greek 'χάος' (khaos) meaning the primordial void or abyss

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: Corresponds to the Ein Sof in Kabbalah, the Tao in Eastern philosophy, and the prima materia in alchemy

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Chaos — the male querent (significator)
sections.Upright: The male consultant himself: the matter as it now stands, full of unformed potential. The fertile void before the question takes shape.
sections.Reversed: A question asked from confusion or anxiety; the void felt as emptiness rather than promise.
sections.Etymology: Chaos / The Void. From Greek 'χάος' (khaos) meaning the primordial void or abyss. Represents the male querent (significator). In Hermetic philosophy, this is the Ein Sof or Tao—divine consciousness before manifestation.
sections.Historical Context:
In Etteilla's system, Le Chaos represents the first moment of the Genesis creation narrative—the primordial void before 'Let there be light.' This card draws heavily from the Hermetic text 'Poimandres' (Divine Pymander), where creation begins with an infinite darkness containing all potential.

Jean-Baptiste Alliette (Etteilla) believed tarot originated with the Egyptian priests of the Temple of Ptah at Memphis, encoding ancient wisdom about cosmic origins. The Chaos card embodies this philosophy: it is not emptiness but pregnant potential, the divine spirit before it takes form.

In practical cartomancy, this card serves as the male querent's significator—representing the questioner himself when male. Etteilla's 1783 'Manière de se récréer avec le jeu de cartes nommées tarots' established this convention, which persists in Grand Etteilla readings today.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Corresponds to the Ein Sof in Kabbalah, the Tao in Eastern philosophy, and the prima materia in alchemy
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **No Marseille equivalent.** This is a *new* trump heading a creation sequence Etteilla claimed to read off the Temple of Memphis; nothing in the TdM corresponds to it, and it carries the querent-significator function the Marseille game deck never had [@web_pdr_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Chaos shows a male figure — the Questioner — surrounded by swirling primordial forms. In the Livre de Thot, this first card represents the male querent who is asking the oracle, and its chaotic surround evokes the undifferentiated void before creation. Etteilla placed this card first to acknowledge the questioner's presence as the starting point of any reading, the self in the middle of what is unknown.
sections.Symbol: The Male Questioner in Chaos — the self at the threshold of the unknown, the human being who initiates the oracle by placing themselves before the void.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:3 'Let there be light' - the first act of creation
etymology.origin: From Latin 'lumen' meaning light, radiance, or understanding

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The Divine Nous (Mind) of Hermetic philosophy; the first emanation of the Absolute

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Lumière — Light / Clarification
sections.Upright: Illumination and clarity — understanding dawning on the matter (Etteilla's 'éclaircissement').
sections.Reversed: Fire and ardour turned destructive; clarity flaring into heat and haste.
sections.Etymology: The Light / Enlightenment. From Latin 'lumen' meaning light, radiance, or understanding. Corresponds to Genesis 1:3 'Let there be light' - the first act of creation
sections.Historical Context:
La Lumière represents the first divine act in Genesis: 'And God said, Let there be light: and there was light.' In Etteilla's Hermetic interpretation, this is not merely physical illumination but the emergence of consciousness and understanding from the void.

The card draws from the Corpus Hermeticum, where light represents divine Nous (Mind) becoming aware of itself. Etteilla's system uniquely places this as card 2, immediately following Chaos, establishing a creation sequence that differs markedly from the Tarot de Marseille tradition.

In the 1843 'Jeu de la princesses' version, this card appears as 'Osiris or the Sun,' connecting to Egyptian solar theology.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The Divine Nous (Mind) of Hermetic philosophy; the first emanation of the Absolute
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **No Marseille equivalent.** A cosmogony card with no TdM trump behind it [@web_pdr_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: La Lumière shows the first emanation of divine light — rays of brightness parting from darkness. This is Etteilla's version of the first day of creation: the separation of light from chaos, the initial act of divine intelligence making the formless into something perceptible. In the Livre de Thot, the image is celestial and expansive, the rays emanating outward as the primordial act of enlightenment.
sections.Symbol: The First Light parting from chaos — divine intelligence as the primal ordering principle, illumination as the first creative act.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:11-12, the creation of vegetation on the third day
etymology.origin: From Latin 'planta' meaning plant, sprout, or cutting

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The vegetable soul; the life force animating organic matter

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Les Plantes — Growth / Discourse
sections.Upright: What is proposed takes root; growth, talk that bears fruit, the green beginning of a thing.
sections.Reversed: Things still fluid and unsettled; words that run off like water, nothing yet rooted.
sections.Etymology: The Plants / Vegetation. From Latin 'planta' meaning plant, sprout, or cutting. Corresponds to Genesis 1:11-12, the creation of vegetation on the third day
sections.Historical Context:
Les Plantes embodies the third day of Genesis creation, when God commanded the earth to bring forth grass, herbs, and fruit-bearing trees. In Etteilla's system, this represents the first manifestation of organic life—the transition from abstract elements to living, growing things.

The Hermetic interpretation connects this card to the vegetable soul in Neoplatonic philosophy—the animating principle that distinguishes living matter from mere elements. Etteilla saw in vegetation the first evidence of divine life force manifesting in material form.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The vegetable soul; the life force animating organic matter
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **No Marseille equivalent** [@web_pdr_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Les Plantes depicts the emergence of vegetation from the earth — the second day of Etteilla's Genesis sequence, when plant life covers the ground with green. The image shows leaves, trees, or botanical forms emerging as the world's creative fertility first manifests. Etteilla associated this card with growth, patience, and the fecundity of the natural world.
sections.Symbol: Plants emerging from the earth — the world's creative fertility, growth as the second act of divine ordering.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:6-8, the creation of the firmament
etymology.origin: From Latin 'caelum' meaning sky, heavens, or celestial vault

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The celestial sphere; the realm of planetary intelligences and fixed stars

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Ciel — The Heavens
sections.Upright: Favour from above: expansion, protection, a matter blessed and widening.
sections.Reversed: A blessing withheld or delayed; distance, a sky that does not answer.
sections.Etymology: The Heavens / The Sky. From Latin 'caelum' meaning sky, heavens, or celestial vault. Corresponds to Genesis 1:6-8, the creation of the firmament
sections.Historical Context:
Le Ciel represents the creation of the firmament—the great dome separating the waters above from the waters below. In ancient cosmology, this was understood as the physical structure of heaven itself, the boundary between earthly and divine realms.

In Hermetic philosophy, the celestial sphere contains the planetary intelligences that govern earthly affairs. Etteilla integrated astrological correspondences throughout his system, seeing the heavens as a living influence rather than mere backdrop.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The celestial sphere; the realm of planetary intelligences and fixed stars
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **No Marseille equivalent** [@web_pdr_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Ciel depicts the celestial vault — the heavens as a dome or expanse above the earth. This card represents the firmament of creation, the separation of above and below that structures the world. In the Etteilla system the celestial card is associated with aspiration, vision, and the realm of ideals that exists above the material plane.
sections.Symbol: The Heavens as vault and firmament — the domain of aspiration and vision, the structured sky that separates above from below.

items.name: L'Homme et les Quadrupèdes (Man and Quadrupeds)

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:24-27, creation of land animals and humanity
etymology.literal: Man and the Quadrupeds / Humanity and Animals

metadata.genesis_day: Day 6 - Creation of Animals and Humanity
metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The microcosm; humanity as reflection of cosmic order

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: L'Homme et les Quadrupèdes — Man and Beasts
sections.Upright: The living world of work and livelihood; natural strength, sustenance, the body's labour.
sections.Reversed: The brute in man; appetite unchecked, toil without reward.
sections.Etymology: Man and the Quadrupeds / Humanity and Animals. From Latin 'homo' (man) and 'quadrupes' (four-footed animal). Corresponds to Genesis 1:24-27, creation of land animals and humanity
sections.Genesis Connection: Day 6 - Creation of Animals and Humanity
sections.Historical Context:
This card depicts the sixth day of creation, when God made the beasts of the earth and then humanity in His own image. In Etteilla's system, this represents the culmination of material creation—the emergence of conscious beings capable of reflecting the divine.

The Hermetic tradition sees humanity as the microcosm—containing within itself all the elements and principles of the greater cosmos. Etteilla's card reflects this: the human figure stands as the meeting point of heaven and earth, spirit and matter.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The microcosm; humanity as reflection of cosmic order
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **No Marseille equivalent** [@web_pdr_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: L'Homme et les Quadrupèdes shows the human figure alongside four-legged animals in the scene of creation. Man and beast together on the earth — the fifth day of Etteilla's sequence — represents the world populated with its creatures, the human standing among and over the animal kingdom. The card is associated with the earthly partnership between human intelligence and animal nature.
sections.Symbol: Man and four-legged animals in creation — the human being in relation to the animal world, intelligence and instinct cohabiting the created earth.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:14-19, creation of sun, moon, and stars
etymology.origin: From Latin 'stella' meaning star or celestial body

metadata.genesis_day: Day 4 - Creation of Sun, Moon, and Stars
metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The stellar intelligences; astrological influences on human affairs

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Les Astres — The Stars
sections.Upright: The stars favourable: hope, guidance, a destiny inclined to the good.
sections.Reversed: An ill or clouded star; hope deferred, a guiding light obscured.
sections.Etymology: The Stars. From Latin 'stella' meaning star or celestial body. Corresponds to Genesis 1:14-19, creation of sun, moon, and stars
sections.Genesis Connection: Day 4 - Creation of Sun, Moon, and Stars
sections.Historical Context:
Les Étoiles depicts the fourth day of Genesis, when God created the sun, moon, and stars to govern day and night and to mark seasons, days, and years. For Etteilla, this was not merely astronomical but deeply astrological—the stars influence human destiny.

Etteilla's 'Société des Interprètes du Livre de Thot' systematically integrated astrology with tarot. The stars card embodies this synthesis: celestial bodies are not distant objects but active powers shaping earthly events.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The stellar intelligences; astrological influences on human affairs
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **No clean Marseille equivalent.** Imagery overlaps the TdM **Star** (XVII), but Etteilla's card is a *creation*-of-the-luminaries card, not the Marseille Star figure; treat the overlap as thematic only (confidence: low on any one-to-one mapping) [@web_pdr_etteilla; @web_etteillastrumps]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Les Astres shows the arrangement of celestial bodies — sun, moon, and stars placed in their positions in the firmament. This card represents the ordering of the heavens, the celestial mechanics that govern time and season. In the Livre de Thot, the celestial bodies are depicted as luminous forms in a starry vault, each in its proper place in the cosmic order.
sections.Symbol: The Celestial Bodies in their positions — cosmic order made visible, the stars and planets arranged in their governance of time.

items.name: Les Oiseaux et les Poissons (Birds and Fishes)

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:20-23, creation of sea and sky creatures on day five

metadata.genesis_day: Day 5 - Creation of Birds and Fish
metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The elemental realms of Air and Water; mobility between states

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Les Oiseaux et les Poissons — Birds and Fishes
sections.Upright: Air and water teeming: news, messages, plenty, journeys, fertile movement.
sections.Reversed: Idle gossip, scattered news, a voyage or message delayed.
sections.Etymology: The Birds and the Fish. From Latin 'avis' (bird) and 'piscis' (fish). Corresponds to Genesis 1:20-23, creation of sea and sky creatures on day five
sections.Genesis Connection: Day 5 - Creation of Birds and Fish
sections.Historical Context:
Les Oiseaux et les Poissons represents the fifth day of creation, when God filled the waters with fish and the sky with birds. These are the first animals—creatures that inhabit the fluid realms of water and air rather than solid earth.

In Etteilla's Hermetic symbolism, birds and fish represent mobility and freedom in their respective elements. Birds ascend toward the divine; fish explore the depths of the unconscious.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The elemental realms of Air and Water; mobility between states
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **No Marseille equivalent** [@web_pdr_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Les Oiseaux et les Poissons shows birds in the air and fish in the water — the creatures of the in-between realms, neither fully earthly nor fully celestial. In Etteilla's Genesis sequence this card represents the animation of the atmosphere and the deep waters with life. Birds occupy the middle air and fish the dark below, together completing the spectrum of created life.
sections.Symbol: Birds in air and fish in water — the animated middle realms, life occupying the spaces between earth and heaven.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 2:2-3, the seventh day when God rested and made it holy
etymology.origin: From Latin 'repausare' meaning to rest, pause, or cease from labor

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The Sabbath; sacred rest; return to unity

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Repos — Rest
sections.Upright: The seventh day: rest, completion, peace earned after labour.
sections.Reversed: Idleness and stagnation; rest curdled into inertia.
sections.Etymology: Rest / Repose. From Latin 'repausare' meaning to rest, pause, or cease from labor. Corresponds to Genesis 2:2-3, the seventh day when God rested and made it holy
sections.Historical Context:
Le Repos represents the Sabbath—the seventh day when God rested from creation and sanctified rest itself. In Etteilla's system, this card serves as the female querent's significator, balancing Le Chaos which represents male questioners.

The 1865 Etteilla III booklet states: 'The true sense of this card is profound peace.' This is not mere inactivity but sacred completion—the rest that comes after work is finished.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The Sabbath; sacred rest; return to unity
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **No Marseille equivalent.** A cosmogony trump that *also* carries the female-querent function the TdM never had. (Corrects the repo grammar, which mislabels Card 78 / La Folie as the female significator.) [@web_pdr_etteilla; @web_etteillastrumps]. 

*Sources:* [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Repos depicts a figure or scene of rest — the Sabbath day after the completion of creation. In Etteilla's system this card is not merely cessation but sacred rest, the divine pause that consecrates what has been made. The image in the Livre de Thot shows a figure in repose, the creative work completed and the world given over to a period of contemplation and rest.
sections.Symbol: Sacred rest after creation — the divine pause that completes the creative cycle, contemplation as the final act of the week's making.

etymology.notes: One of the four cardinal virtues from Plato's Republic
etymology.origin: From Latin 'justitia' meaning righteousness, equity, or lawfulness

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: Ma'at; cosmic balance; the weighing of souls

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Justice — Justice
sections.Upright: Lawful judgment, balance restored, a matter set right.
sections.Reversed: Injustice, bias, the law bent or misused.
sections.Etymology: Justice. From Latin 'justitia' meaning righteousness, equity, or lawfulness. One of the four cardinal virtues from Plato's Republic
sections.Historical Context:
Following the seven days of creation, Etteilla's trumps transition to the four cardinal virtues as described by Plato: Justice, Temperance, Fortitude, and Prudence. La Justice represents the first of these—the principle of righteous judgment and fair dealing.

In the Hermetic tradition, Justice connects to Ma'at—the Egyptian goddess of truth, balance, and cosmic order.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Ma'at; cosmic balance; the weighing of souls
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Justice (VIII)** — one of the few trumps Etteilla keeps recognisable — but renumbered to **9** and re-read as one of his four cardinal virtues (cards 9–12) [@web_etteillastrumps]. 

*Sources:* [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: La Justice appears as the fourth cardinal virtue in the Etteilla sequence, depicted as a crowned or robed female figure with sword and scales. Etteilla placed Justice at position nine rather than at the Marseille position, incorporating the virtue into his revised allegorical sequence. The image follows the classical iconography of Justice as the weigher of deeds.
sections.Symbol: Justice with sword and scales at position 9 — the virtue of equity and measure, the classical personification of law as divine principle.

etymology.notes: The second cardinal virtue; the golden mean between excess and deficiency
etymology.origin: From Latin 'temperantia' meaning self-control, moderation, or sobriety

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The alchemical mixing of elements; the art of proportion

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Tempérance — Temperance
sections.Upright: Moderation and prudent management; opposites blended well.
sections.Reversed: Excess and imbalance; the measure lost.
sections.Etymology: Temperance / Moderation. From Latin 'temperantia' meaning self-control, moderation, or sobriety. The second cardinal virtue; the golden mean between excess and deficiency
sections.Historical Context:
La Tempérance represents the second Platonic virtue—the principle of moderation and self-control that enables harmonious living. The traditional image shows a figure pouring liquid between vessels, representing the careful mixing and balancing of elements.

This alchemical symbolism was not lost on Etteilla, who saw in temperance the secret of transformation.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The alchemical mixing of elements; the art of proportion
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Temperance (XIIII)**, renumbered to **10** and grouped as a cardinal virtue [@web_etteillastrumps]. 

*Sources:* [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: La Tempérance appears as the cardinal virtue of moderation at position ten, typically depicted as a female figure pouring liquid between two vessels. Etteilla's Tempérance follows the conventional allegorical personification of this virtue, the measured exchange between two containers representing balance and self-regulation.
sections.Symbol: Temperance pouring between vessels at position 10 — the virtue of moderation and measured exchange, self-regulation as a divine principle.

etymology.notes: The third cardinal virtue; moral courage rather than mere physical power
etymology.origin: From Latin 'fortitudo' meaning courage, bravery, or strength of character

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: Mastery of the animal nature; courage guided by wisdom

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Force — Strength
sections.Upright: Courage and self-mastery; force rightly governed.
sections.Reversed: Weakness, or strength misused as domination.
sections.Etymology: Strength / Fortitude. From Latin 'fortitudo' meaning courage, bravery, or strength of character. The third cardinal virtue; moral courage rather than mere physical power
sections.Historical Context:
La Force embodies the third Platonic virtue—fortitude or courage. This is not mere physical strength but moral courage: the capacity to face difficulty, danger, or pain without yielding to fear.

The traditional image often shows a figure with a lion, representing the mastery of animal instincts through gentle strength rather than violent domination.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Mastery of the animal nature; courage guided by wisdom
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Strength/Fortitude (XI)**, renumbered to **11** as a cardinal virtue [@web_etteillastrumps]. 

*Sources:* [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: La Force appears at position eleven as the virtue of fortitude — a figure subduing a lion or overcoming a physical challenge through courage and patient strength. Etteilla's Force follows the traditional personification of this virtue, the human mastery of instinct expressed as gentle physical dominance.
sections.Symbol: Strength subduing the lion at position 11 — the virtue of fortitude, courage as patient dominance rather than violent conquest.

etymology.notes: The fourth cardinal virtue; considered by Aristotle as 'practical wisdom'
etymology.origin: From Latin 'prudentia' meaning foresight, wisdom, or practical judgment

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: Phronesis; practical wisdom; the inner counselor

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Prudence — Prudence
sections.Upright: Foresight and careful counsel (Etteilla set Prudence where ordinary tarot has the Hanged Man).
sections.Reversed: Imprudence and rashness; a clear warning ignored.
sections.Etymology: Prudence / Wisdom. From Latin 'prudentia' meaning foresight, wisdom, or practical judgment. The fourth cardinal virtue; considered by Aristotle as 'practical wisdom'
sections.Historical Context:
La Prudence completes the quartet of cardinal virtues—representing practical wisdom, foresight, and good judgment. Aristotle called this 'phronesis,' the ability to discern the right course of action in particular circumstances.

The traditional image often shows a figure with a mirror (self-knowledge) and a serpent (wisdom).
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Phronesis; practical wisdom; the inner counselor
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Hanged Man (XII)** — Etteilla's most ingenious substitution. Turning the Hanged Man upside-down and reading the rope as a serpent yields an image of Prudence ("there are snakes in that grass"); he changed the figure's gender and renamed it to complete the quartet of virtues. The Hanged Man as a distinct trump **disappears** from the deck [@web_etteillastrumps]. 

*Sources:* [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: La Prudence represents the fourth classical virtue at position twelve in the Etteilla sequence — the virtue of wisdom and foresight, associated with the mirror (self-knowledge) and the serpent (wisdom). Etteilla's inclusion of Prudence as a separate card is one of his major departures from the Marseille tradition, which lacks Prudence among its four virtues.
sections.Symbol: Prudence with mirror and serpent at position 12 — the virtue of foresight and self-knowledge, wisdom that looks before it acts.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Grand Prêtre — The High Priest. Despite the 'priest' title, this is Etteilla's reworking of the Marseille **Lovers (VI)**: its divinatory sense is *Mariage / Union* — the priest who sanctions the marriage.
sections.Upright: Counsel, sanction, marriage, spiritual authority; a trusted guide.
sections.Reversed: Hollow dogma, a bad counsellor, hypocrisy in high place.
sections.Correction: Correction (2026-06-13, research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md): mapped here to the Tarot de Marseille trump this card actually reworks; earlier metadata mis-mapped it. Sources: etteillastrumps (M. J. Hurst), Public Domain Review, benebell wen.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Lovers / L'Amoureux (VI)**, renumbered to **13** and narrowed to "Marriage/Union" (Papus and the Etteilla tradition correlate it to the Lovers) [@web_etteillastrumps; @web_benebell_etteilla]. **— Corrects the repo grammar, which maps Card 13 to the Hierophant/High Priest.** The "High Priest" title is a posthumous Etteilla II/III re-labelling of the *image*; the card's identity is the Lovers, its meaning marriage. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Grand Prêtre (The High Priest) represents Etteilla's equivalent of the Hierophant or Pope — an enthroned spiritual authority with sacred vestments and sceptre, presiding over a scene of religious instruction or blessing. The title 'Grand Prêtre' emphasizes priestly rather than papal authority, the intermediary between divine and human realms.
sections.Symbol: The High Priest enthroned at position 13 — sacred intermediary between divine knowledge and human understanding, the keeper of religious tradition.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Diable — Force / The Devil
sections.Upright: Raw force and vehemence — power over circumstance (Etteilla read the Devil as energy, not only evil).
sections.Reversed: Fury and ravage; bondage to one's own passions.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Devil (XV)**, renumbered to **14** and re-conceived as impersonal *force/necessity* rather than only "evil" — Etteilla reads the Devil as energy [@web_etteillastrumps]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Diable appears at position fourteen in the Etteilla system as the figure of infernal temptation and bondage — typically depicted with horns and wings, a pedestal with bound or chained figures below. Etteilla's Devil card follows the conventional allegorical type while placing it earlier in his renumbered sequence than in Marseille tradition.
sections.Symbol: The Devil with bound figures at position 14 — infernal temptation and material bondage, the force that chains through appetite and illusion.

items.name: Le Magicien ou le Bateleur (The Magician/Juggler)

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Magicien / Le Bateleur — Illness
sections.Upright: The juggler's hand at work; in Etteilla's system the Bateleur governs illness and its cure — a matter of the body, skill applied.
sections.Reversed: A charlatan, trickery; malady worsening, a false remedy.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Bateleur / Magician (I)** — the *first* Marseille trump — but Etteilla demotes it to **15** and assigns it the wholly new meaning of **Illness**, severing it from "skill/the conjuror as beginning" [@web_etteillastrumps]. 

*Sources:* [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Magicien ou le Bateleur appears at position fifteen in the Etteilla system, the conjurer or Magician equivalent. In the Etteilla sequence the Magician appears late among the allegorical cards rather than first, his skill and mastery positioned as mature achievement rather than initial promise. The image shows a figure at a table with the tools of his art.
sections.Symbol: The Magician at his table at position 15 — skilled mastery placed in its mature context, the conjurer's ability as achievement rather than potential.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Jugement Dernier — Last Judgement
sections.Upright: Awakening, a decisive change, renewal answering the trumpet's call.
sections.Reversed: A verdict postponed; fear of change, the summons unheeded.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Judgement (XX)**, renumbered to **16**; iconography and core meaning largely preserved [@web_etteillastrumps; @web_benebell_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Jugement Dernier (The Last Judgment) shows the divine angel with trumpet summoning the risen dead — a conventional allegorical rendering of the Christian eschatological scene. In Etteilla's system the Judgment appears at position sixteen, integrated into the revised allegorical sequence between the Magician and Death.
sections.Symbol: The Angel of Judgment with trumpet at position 16 — the divine summons, the call that wakens the dead and completes the cycle of time.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Mort — Death
sections.Upright: Transformation, the necessary end that clears the ground.
sections.Reversed: Inertia; a change resisted, an ending only half-made.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Death (XIII)** — and this is **the famous renumbering**. In every Marseille deck Death is trump XIII (usually *unnamed*); Etteilla moves it to **17** and titles it "Mortalité," deliberately breaking the number-13 association the later tradition treats as fixed [@web_etteillastrumps; @web_aeclectic_etteilla]. *(The repo prompt frames this as "13 → a different position"; the destination is 17. The repo grammar already has 17 correct.)*. 

*Sources:* [@web_aeclectic_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: La Mort shows the skeleton with scythe harvesting the field of the living. Death appears at position seventeen in the Etteilla sequence, the conventional allegorical figure of mortality positioned mid-sequence. The image follows the standard European iconography of Death as the universal harvester.
sections.Symbol: Death's skeleton with scythe at position 17 — the universal harvester, mortality as the inescapable end of every sequence.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Capucin — The Traitor / Hermit
sections.Upright: The hooded solitary; in Etteilla's reading the Capucin is the traitor — concealment, a matter hidden, or prudent withdrawal.
sections.Reversed: A secret enemy unmasked; treachery, false counsel exposed.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Hermit (VIIII)**, renumbered to **18** and re-cast as the **Capuchin** with a strong added sense of concealment/treachery [@web_etteillastrumps]. 

*Sources:* [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Capucin (The Friar/Hermit) shows a monk or Capuchin friar in a cowl and habit, a figure of religious withdrawal and contemplation. Etteilla renamed the Hermit as a Capucin, giving the card a more specifically French monastic character — the Capuchin order being a visible presence in 18th-century French religious life. The figure holds a lantern or staff.
sections.Symbol: The Capuchin Friar at position 18 — religious withdrawal and contemplative solitude, the monk who carries inner light within his cloistered world.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Temple Foudroyé — The Struck Temple
sections.Upright: The temple thrown down: sudden ruin, downfall, a structure broken open.
sections.Reversed: A calamity narrowly escaped — but oppression, confinement, ruin deferred.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Tower / La Maison Dieu (XVI)**, renumbered to **19** and re-titled the "struck Temple," with the Egyptian-temple framing [@web_etteillastrumps; @web_benebell_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Temple Foudroyé (The Struck Temple) shows a classical or Egyptian temple or column struck by lightning, with figures falling. Etteilla's version of the Tower card refers specifically to a temple rather than a tower, evoking the destruction of sacred architecture — perhaps resonating with the French Enlightenment's complicated relationship to the Church and its institutions.
sections.Symbol: The Lightning-struck Temple at position 19 — sacred architecture shattered by celestial force, the fall of institutional religion under the blow of divine rupture.

items.name: La Roue de Fortune (Wheel of Fortune)

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Roue de Fortune — Wheel of Fortune
sections.Upright: Fortune turning upward: increase, elevation, luck in the matter.
sections.Reversed: A fall; fortune reversed, gain turning to loss.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Wheel of Fortune (X)**, renumbered to **20**; meaning largely preserved [@web_etteillastrumps; @web_benebell_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: La Roue de Fortune shows the conventional spinning wheel of Fortune with ascending and descending figures. At position twenty in the Etteilla sequence, the Wheel is positioned late in the allegorical series, Fortune's arbitrariness placed in a context already structured by the virtues and the allegorical narrative.
sections.Symbol: The Wheel of Fortune at position 20 — cyclical rise and fall, Fortune's mechanical indifference placed in the context of Etteilla's revised allegorical sequence.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Despote Africain — The Sovereign
sections.Upright: Great power and a powerful protector; dissension mastered by a strong hand.
sections.Reversed: Tyranny; a dangerous man, discord stirred from above.
sections.Correction: Correction (2026-06-13, research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md): mapped here to the Tarot de Marseille trump this card actually reworks; earlier metadata mis-mapped it. Sources: etteillastrumps (M. J. Hurst), Public Domain Review, benebell wen. (Card 21 'Le Despote Africain' answers to the Chariot (VII); later Grimaud editions title it 'Dissension'.)
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Chariot (VII)** — *not* the Emperor — which Etteilla reworks into a "despotic sovereign" at **21** (benebell wen titles her reconstruction of this key "The Tyrant Embodied in The Chariot Card (Etteilla Tarot Key 21)") [@web_benebell_etteilla]. **— Corrects the repo grammar, which maps Card 21 to the Emperor.** (The Emperor's authority material is dispersed elsewhere in Etteilla's reworking; there is no clean single "Emperor" trump — confidence: medium on the Chariot correspondence, low on any Emperor mapping.). 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteillastrumps].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: Le Despote Africain (The African Despot) is Etteilla's most remarkable and culturally specific trump — a crowned African or dark-complexioned ruler who replaces the World card at the apex of the allegorical sequence. This card has been identified as reflecting 18th-century French orientalism and the deck's claimed Egyptian origins, placing an exotic sovereign at the summit of the ordering sequence.
sections.Symbol: The African Despot crowned at position 21 — the apex sovereign of Etteilla's allegorical sequence, the foreign king who rules where the Marseille World dancer once danced.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Bâtons (Batons) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (enterprise, work, the countryside and growth). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): An enterprising, honourable man — of the country or of business; a good friend in practical affairs.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): The same man turned harsh, grasping, or unreliable in his dealings.
sections.Scene: Roi de Bâton (King of Wands) shows a crowned or enthroned male figure associated with the wand suit — a king who embodies the fiery, creative, and enterprising qualities of the baton. In the Etteilla system the King of Wands is associated with creative authority and entrepreneurial leadership, a figure who commands through vision and enthusiasm.
sections.Symbol: King of Wands — creative authority and entrepreneurial leadership, the king who commands through vision and passionate enterprise.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Bâtons (Batons) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (enterprise, work, the countryside and growth). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): A capable, honest woman of warmth and industry; a good helper and counsellor.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): A meddling or obstinate woman; help offered with strings.
sections.Scene: Reine de Bâton (Queen of Wands) shows a crowned female figure associated with the wand suit — a queen of warmth, creativity, and passionate generosity. In the Etteilla system the Queen of Wands embodies the feminine expression of the suit's fire: nurturing creativity, enthusiastic support of others, and the warmth that sustains creative endeavors.
sections.Symbol: Queen of Wands — warmth and creative nurturing, the feminine expression of the suit's fire as generous and enthusiastic support.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Bâtons (Batons) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (enterprise, work, the countryside and growth). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): A departure, a young man setting out; enterprise on the move, news of work.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): A hasty or quarrelsome youth; a journey or venture gone wrong.
sections.Scene: Chevalier de Bâton (Knight of Wands) shows a mounted figure in action, the wand suit's knight carrying the suit's fiery energy into active pursuit. In the Etteilla system the Knight of Wands is the messenger or pursuer of creative enterprise — swift, enthusiastic, and perhaps impetuous in the pursuit of what fires him.
sections.Symbol: Knight of Wands in pursuit — the swift messenger of creative enterprise, enthusiasm and impulsiveness as the mode of the suit's active rank.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Bâtons (Batons) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (enterprise, work, the countryside and growth). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): A messenger, a youth bearing news of work or family; a small good beginning.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): Bad news, a careless messenger, a rumour about one's affairs.
sections.Scene: Valet de Bâton (Page of Wands) shows a youthful standing figure with the wand as his attribute — the suit's page or messenger, a young person of creative curiosity and eager enthusiasm. In the Etteilla system the Page of Wands brings news of creative ventures and embodies the beginner's energy of open, exploratory enthusiasm.
sections.Symbol: Page of Wands — youthful creative curiosity, the eager bearer of new ventures and open enthusiasm.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Ten of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: the matter at its fullest — completion, or surfeit, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: Excess and burden; a cycle that overstays.
sections.Scene: Dix de Bâton (Ten of Wands) shows ten wand pip symbols in the Etteilla convention — ten batons arranged in the card's field, often with keywords for their divinatory meanings printed in the borders. The ten of wands in the Etteilla system is associated with overload and burden, the weight of accumulated creative obligation.
sections.Symbol: Ten Wands — accumulated creative burden, the weight of multiple obligations at the suit's maximum pip count.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Nine of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: near-fulfilment; intensity, the thing almost complete, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: Strain, delay, intensity turned against itself.
sections.Scene: Neuf de Bâton (Nine of Wands) shows nine wand pip symbols with associated divinatory keywords. The nine of wands in the Etteilla system is associated with resilience and defensive strength — the prepared position of one who has faced many challenges and stands ready for the next.
sections.Symbol: Nine Wands — resilience and defensive strength, the prepared position of the one who has faced many challenges.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Eight of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: comings and goings, change, the matter in motion, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: Movement stalled; restlessness without progress.
sections.Scene: Huit de Bâton (Eight of Wands) shows eight wand pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The eight of wands in the Etteilla system is associated with swift communication and rapid movement — the velocity of messages and events in flight.
sections.Symbol: Eight Wands — swift communication and rapid movement, messages and events in rapid transit.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Seven of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: a hope and a partial success, still uncertain, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: The hope dashed; effort abandoned.
sections.Scene: Sept de Bâton (Seven of Wands) shows seven wand pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The seven of wands in the Etteilla system is associated with courage in the face of opposition — standing one's ground against multiple challengers.
sections.Symbol: Seven Wands — courage against opposition, the solo stand in the face of multiple challenges.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Six of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: a passage, a road, fair give-and-take, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: The way blocked; an exchange gone unequal.
sections.Scene: Six de Bâton (Six of Wands) shows six wand pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The six of wands in the Etteilla system is associated with victory and public recognition — the triumphant return after a creative achievement.
sections.Symbol: Six Wands — victory and public recognition, the triumphant display of creative achievement.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Five of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: a turn or loss within the matter — a test, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: The loss easing, or the test deepening.
sections.Scene: Cinq de Bâton (Five of Wands) shows five wand pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The five of wands in the Etteilla system is associated with conflict and competition — the contest of multiple creative energies in active opposition.
sections.Symbol: Five Wands — conflict and competition, the contest of multiple creative energies in active opposition.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Four of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: a settling, a small security, a needed pause, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: Stagnation; holding on too tightly.
sections.Scene: Quatre de Bâton (Four of Wands) shows four wand pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The four of wands in the Etteilla system is associated with celebration and homecoming — the stable structure of achieved creative success, a foundation for festivity.
sections.Symbol: Four Wands — celebration and homecoming, the stable structure of creative success as a foundation for festivity.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Three of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: increase; a third party, growth, the thing taking shape, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: Growth checked; effort scattered, a delay.
sections.Scene: Trois de Bâton (Three of Wands) shows three wand pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The three of wands in the Etteilla system is associated with foresight and enterprise — the visionary look toward the horizon, creative momentum directed outward.
sections.Symbol: Three Wands — foresight and enterprise, the visionary gaze directed outward toward creative expansion.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Two of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: a meeting or exchange; the matter shared with another, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: Division, a pairing strained or broken.
sections.Scene: Deux de Bâton (Two of Wands) shows two wand pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The two of wands in the Etteilla system is associated with choice and personal power — the individual who holds the world in their hands, the creative will at a point of decision.
sections.Symbol: Two Wands — choice and personal power, creative will at the point of decision between two paths.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Ace of Bâtons (Batons) — the suit of Fire, governing enterprise, work, the countryside and growth.
sections.Upright: a strong onset of the matter — the suit's force at its purest, within enterprise.
sections.Reversed: That force blocked or misdirected at the very start.
sections.Scene: As de Bâton (Ace of Wands) shows a single wand pip as the suit's foundational emblem. In the Etteilla system the Ace of Wands is the concentrated essence of the suit's creative fire — the seed of enterprise, the initial spark of creative impulse before it divides into specific projects.
sections.Symbol: Ace of Wands — the undivided creative fire, the initial spark of enterprise and creative impulse.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Coupes (Cups) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (love, affection, friendship and the heart). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): A fair, kindly, well-meaning man — often a man of feeling or of the Church; a benefactor in love or friendship.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): A fickle or self-indulgent man; affection that cannot be trusted.
sections.Scene: Roi de Coupe (King of Cups) shows a crowned or enthroned male figure associated with the cup suit — a king who embodies emotional maturity, wisdom in feeling, and the patient governance of the inner life. In the Etteilla system the King of Cups is a figure of professional and emotional authority, one who balances intellect and feeling.
sections.Symbol: King of Cups — emotional authority and wisdom in feeling, the king who governs his inner life with patience and professional maturity.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Coupes (Cups) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (love, affection, friendship and the heart). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): A loving, fair-natured woman; devotion, friendship, a tender influence.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): A jealous or capricious woman; love soured into possessiveness.
sections.Scene: Reine de Coupe (Queen of Cups) shows a crowned female figure associated with the cup suit — a queen of emotional intelligence, intuition, and compassionate understanding. In the Etteilla system the Queen of Cups embodies the feminine expression of the suit's water: deep feeling, creative imagination, and the capacity to understand others through empathy.
sections.Symbol: Queen of Cups — emotional intelligence and compassionate understanding, the feminine expression of the suit's depth of feeling.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Coupes (Cups) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (love, affection, friendship and the heart). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): An arrival, a lover or friend approaching; an invitation, a proposal of the heart.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): A seducer or flatterer; an arrival that disappoints.
sections.Scene: Chevalier de Coupe (Knight of Cups) shows a mounted figure bearing a cup — the suit's active messenger of emotional and romantic quests. In the Etteilla system the Knight of Cups arrives with an emotional invitation or romantic proposal, a figure who carries the suit's feeling forward on the wings of imagination.
sections.Symbol: Knight of Cups bearing a cup — the romantic messenger or emotional invitation, feeling carried forward through the suit's active rank.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Coupes (Cups) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (love, affection, friendship and the heart). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): A fair youth bringing affection or good news; a sweet, sincere proposal.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): A flatterer, an idle promise; feeling that is only on the surface.
sections.Scene: Valet de Coupe (Page of Cups) shows a youthful figure with the cup as attribute — a sensitive young person of imaginative and artistic inclinations. In the Etteilla system the Page of Cups brings messages of an emotional or creative nature, a young person attuned to the inner world and receptive to feeling.
sections.Symbol: Page of Cups — youthful emotional sensitivity and imaginative receptivity, the bearer of creative and emotional messages.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Ten of Coupes (Cups) — the suit of Water, governing love, affection, friendship and the heart.
sections.Upright: the matter at its fullest — completion, or surfeit, within love.
sections.Reversed: Excess and burden; a cycle that overstays.
sections.Scene: Dix de Coupe (Ten of Cups) shows ten cup pip symbols with divinatory keywords. In the Etteilla system the ten of cups is associated with lasting happiness and family harmony — the emotional suit at its most fulfilled and domestic.
sections.Symbol: Ten Cups — lasting happiness and family harmony, the suit at its most emotionally fulfilled and domestically complete.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Nine of Coupes (Cups) — the suit of Water, governing love, affection, friendship and the heart.
sections.Upright: near-fulfilment; intensity, the thing almost complete, within love.
sections.Reversed: Strain, delay, intensity turned against itself.
sections.Scene: Neuf de Coupe (Nine of Cups) shows nine cup pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The nine of cups in the Etteilla system is associated with wishes fulfilled and satisfaction — the contented state of one whose emotional desires have been met.
sections.Symbol: Nine Cups — wishes fulfilled and satisfaction, emotional desires met at the suit's near-maximum register.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Eight of Coupes (Cups) — the suit of Water, governing love, affection, friendship and the heart.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Épées (Swords) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (conflict, law, arms and sharp trouble). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Folie — the Fool (0), the unnumbered card left outside the running count. (Not the female querent: that significator is Card 8, Le Repos.)
sections.Upright: The wild card outside the count: the matter unbound, open to any turn; the holy fool, divine madness.
sections.Reversed: Recklessness; a question asked in folly, energy with no rein.
sections.Correction: Correction (2026-06-13): earlier metadata mislabelled La Folie as 'The Questioner – Female'. La Folie is the Fool; the female querent significator is Card 8 (Le Repos). Sources: stolen-thyme Etteilla trumps, benebell wen, Public Domain Review.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** answers to the **Marseille Fool (unnumbered)**. Etteilla's innovation is to assign it the **number 0** and the title **"La Folie"** — reportedly the first deck to do both [@web_etteilla_org]. **— Corrects the repo grammar, which labels this the "female querent."** La Folie is the Fool; the female significator is Card 8 (Le Repos) [@web_pdr_etteilla; @web_etteilla_org]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_etteilla_org], [@web_pdr_etteilla].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-i-livre-de-thot.md).
sections.Scene: La Folie (The Fool) is Etteilla's version of the unnumbered Fool, placed at card 78 — the deck's last card number — rather than at the beginning or end of the trump sequence. The Fool appears as a conventionally wandering or comic figure, but Etteilla's placement of the Folie at 78 reflects his systematic renumbering of the entire tarot.
sections.Symbol: The Fool at card 78 — the unnumbered placed at the final number, folly as the last card in Etteilla's systematic sequence.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “balance” (6).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with the divinatory theme of Balance in the Etteilla system — the quality of equilibrium between opposing forces, the measure of just proportion. Balance in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Cardinal Virtue of Justice, with the two-cup Temperance, and with pip cards in the middle range of the suits.
sections.Symbol: Balance — the equilibrium between opposing forces, justice and measure as the achieved state of the consulting oracle.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “creativity” (5).

Creativity in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Wands suit and with the early Genesis cards' depiction of the creative acts of divine order.
sections.Symbol: Creativity — the generative impulse of invention and artistic expression, associated with the suit of fire and the creative acts of the Genesis sequence.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “truth” (5).

Truth in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Cardinal Virtues, with the Swords suit's cutting clarity, and with the oracle's fundamental purpose of revealing what is actually the case.
sections.Symbol: Truth — accurate perception and honest expression, the fundamental aim of the Etteilla oracle.

sections.Symbol: Patience — the quality of enduring and allowing things to unfold, associated with the methodical suit of coins and the temporal ordering of creation.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “security” (4).

sections.Symbol: Wisdom — deep understanding forged from experience, the mature knowledge that the oracle channels for the questioner.

sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Clarity in the Etteilla system — the quality of clear perception, unobstructed understanding, and the removal of confusion or illusion. Clarity in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Swords suit's mental sharpness and with the oracle's function of illuminating what is obscure.
sections.Symbol: Clarity — unobstructed understanding and clear perception, the quality the oracle provides by cutting through confusion.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “guidance” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Guidance in the Etteilla system — the direction provided by the oracle, by authority figures, or by inner wisdom. Guidance in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the High Priest and other figures of spiritual and social authority, as well as with the oracle itself as a tool for navigating uncertain situations.
sections.Symbol: Guidance — the direction provided by authority or inner wisdom, the fundamental service of the Etteilla oracle to the questioner.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “harmony” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Harmony in the Etteilla system — the quality of agreement, peace, and the concordance of disparate elements into a unified whole. Harmony in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the stable mid-range pip cards and with positive resolutions in the reading.
sections.Symbol: Harmony — the agreement and concordance of disparate elements, associated with stable and positive outcomes in the Etteilla reading.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “intuition” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Intuition in the Etteilla system — the direct knowing that bypasses conscious reasoning, the inner voice or felt sense of what is true. Intuition in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Cups suit's emotional intelligence and with the oracle reading itself as an act of non-rational perception.
sections.Symbol: Intuition — direct knowing beyond reasoning, associated with the Cups suit's emotional intelligence and with the oracular process itself.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “manifestation” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Manifestation in the Etteilla system — the process by which inner intentions become outer realities. Manifestation in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the early Genesis cards' depiction of creation and with the Coin suit's domain of practical material results.
sections.Symbol: Manifestation — the process of bringing inner intention into material form, associated with the creative Genesis sequence and the practical Coin suit.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “nurturing” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Nurturing in the Etteilla system — the quality of caring support, the tending of what is growing or vulnerable. Nurturing in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Queen figures across the suits and with the Cups suit's emotional care.
sections.Symbol: Nurturing — caring support for what is growing or vulnerable, associated with the Queen courts and the Cups suit's emotional care.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “planning” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Planning in the Etteilla system — the capacity for forethought, preparation, and the systematic organization of resources toward a goal. Planning in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Prudence trump and with the methodical Coin suit's patient accumulation.
sections.Symbol: Planning — forethought and systematic preparation, associated with the Prudence trump and the Coin suit's patient organization of resources.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “skill” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Skill in the Etteilla system — the developed competence in a particular domain, the mastery acquired through practice. Skill in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Magician trump and with the craftsman-related pip cards in the Coin and Wands suits.
sections.Symbol: Skill — developed competence through practice, associated with the Magician's mastery and the craftsman pip cards across the suits.

sections.Description: Cross-cutting facets derived from shared keywords — the same card appears under every keyword it carries.
sections.Scene: A navigational axis organizing all items in the Etteilla deck by keyword — grouping the cards by their associated divinatory themes and qualities. The keyword axis allows navigation across the deck through themes like balance, creativity, wisdom, and manifestation rather than through suit or trump sequence.
sections.Symbol: The keyword axis — a thematic cross-index of the Etteilla oracle, allowing approach to its 94 items through the qualities and divinatory themes that connect cards across suits and trumps.
